content.text: in Claude's PDF Support is the process of uploading PDF documents to enable deep analysis, text extraction, chart interpretation, and visual content understanding. When you send a PDF, Claude processes it as both text and images for comprehensive understanding.
Sending PDFs means attaching PDF documents to your Claude conversation so the AI can
parse, analyze, and reason over the entire document

1. Open claude.ai or launch mobile app
2. Near the chat box, look for:
   - Plus button (+) or paperclip icon (desktop)
   - "Add files" or "Upload file" option
3. Select your PDF from device:
   - Browse and click "Open"
   - OR drag and drop into chat window
4. PDF attaches to conversation

PDF Upload → System extracts document content
    ↓
1. Convert each page to image
2. Extract text from each page
3. Provide text + page images together
    ↓
Claude analyzes BOTH text and images
    ↓
Responses reference PDF content (when relevant)
What Claude Can Extract from PDFs

- Charts, diagrams, images
- Visual layouts and formatting
- Text and images together (comprehensive understanding)
Can handle larger, but performance varies
Best Use Cases for PDF Analysis

"Extract all figures and their captions from this paper"
"Summarize the methodology and key findings"
"What are the main limitations mentioned?"
Ingests entire document, maintains coherence across long analytical sessions.

"What are the steps for installation?"
"Extract all troubleshooting procedures"
"Summarize the safety warnings"

"Extract all data from charts in this annual report"
"What are the key financial metrics?"
"Summarize the risk factors section"

"Extract all contract terms and dates"
"What are the obligations of each party?"
"Identify any clauses that need revision"

❌ Bad: "What's in this document?"
✅ Good: "Extract the restaurant name, total amount, and date from this receipt PDF"

"I'll scan this document page by page, extracting sections in order:
1. Introduction
2. Methodology
3. Results
4. Conclusion"

"What does page 15 say about risk management?"
"Extract the table from pages 20-22"
Use structured prompts, focus on specific sections
Verify with human oversight for critical info
PDF tasks deplete message limits faster than text

# Upload annual report PDF
messages=[{
    "role": "user",
    "content": [
        {"type": "file", "source": annual_report.pdf},
        {"type": "text", "text": """
Extract all data from charts and graphs:
- Revenue trends over time
- Regional breakdown percentages
- Key metrics with exact values

Return as JSON with categories and values.
If numbers are unclear, return 'UNK'.
"""}
    ]
}]

# Result: Structured extraction with chart data, 
# maintaining accuracy across 100+ pages
When working with sending pdfs, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with sending pdfs. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of sending pdfs scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
